{{Short description|none}}

| Largest loss       = {{nowrap|{{fb|KUW}} 10–0 {{fb-rt|TPE}} <br> ([[Al Ain]], [[United Arab Emirates]], 9 November 2006)}}
| Regional name      = [[AFC Asian Cup|Asian Cup]]
| Regional cup apps  = 2
| Regional cup first = [[1960 AFC Asian Cup|1960]]
| Regional cup best  = Third place ([[1960 AFC Asian Cup|1960]])
| 2ndRegional name   = [[AFC Challenge Cup]] 
| 2ndRegional cup apps = 1
| 2ndRegional cup first = [[2006 AFC Challenge Cup|2006]] 
| 2ndRegional cup best = Quarter-finals (2006)
}}
{{Infobox Chinese
| t = 中華台北男子足球代表隊
| p = Zhōnghuá Táiběi Nánzǐ Zúqiú Dàibiǎo Duì
| w = Chung-hua T'ai-pei Nan-tzŭ Tsu-ch'iu Tai-piao Tui
}}

The '''Chinese Taipei national football team''' ({{langx|zh|中華台北男子足球代表隊}}) represents [[Taiwan]] (The Republic of China) in international [[Association football|football]]<ref>{{cite web |last1=Oyen |first1=Meredith |title=Why Taiwan is competing in the Olympics under 'Chinese Taipei' |url=https://nationalpost.com/news/why-taiwan-is-competing-in-the-olympics-under-chinese-taipei |website=National Post |publisher=Postmedia Network Inc. |access-date=20 July 2023}}</ref> and is controlled by the [[Chinese Taipei Football Association]]. Despite never qualifying for the [[FIFA World Cup]], Chinese Taipei, then known as Republic of China, reached the semi-finals of the [[1960 AFC Asian Cup|1960]] and [[1968 AFC Asian Cup]]s, finishing third in the former. The side also won gold at the [[Football at the 1954 Asian Games|1954]] and [[Football at the 1958 Asian Games|1958 Asian Games]], although the players in the team originated from [[British Hong Kong]].

==History==

=== Formation and early success (1924–1970) ===
{{Further|China national football team#As Republic of China (1913–1949)}}
What is now known as [[Chinese Taipei Football Association]] (CTFA) was originally established in [[mainland China]] in 1924 as the China Football Association ({{zh|t=中華足球聯合會}}). It joined FIFA in 1931. Following the conclusion of the [[Chinese Civil War]] in 1949, the association relocated to Taiwan along with the Republic of China government. On 14 June 1952, FIFA acknowledged that the [[All-China Sports Federation]] ({{zh|t=中華全國體育總會}}), which later established [[Chinese Football Association]] ({{zh|t=中國足球協會}}) was part of, on [[People's Republic of China]] (PRC), not the [[Chinese Taipei Football Association|China Football Association]] located on Taiwan, was the recognized authority over Chinese Football with their membership dating to 1924.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://sports.163.com/07/0319/03/39TS1BEK00051C88.html|title=1949年-1979年中国足球国家队大事记|publisher=sports.163.com|date=19 March 2007|access-date=31 August 2016|archive-date=11 September 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160911113243/http://sports.163.com/07/0319/03/39TS1BEK00051C88.html|url-status=dead}}</ref> Therefore, all the achievements and records of the ROC team up to 1949 were inherited by the [[China national football team|PRC counterpart]].

Taiwan was separately admitted as a member of FIFA in June 1954 over the objections of the CFA and the PRC government at the 29th [[FIFA Congress]] in [[Bern]].<ref name="Homburg">{{cite journal |last=Homburg|first=H. |date=2006 |title=FIFA and the "Chinese Question", 1954-1980: an Exercise of Statutes. |journal=Historical Social Research |publisher=GESIS - Leibniz Institute for the Social Sciences |volume=31 |issue=1 |pages=69–87 |jstor=20762103}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite web |date=28 June 2011 |title=Statistical Kit: Preliminary Draw for the 2014 FIFA World Cup Brazil |url=https://digitalhub.fifa.com/m/52463a2cc01d135/original/c91tgtfetbsmneex1vbc-pdf.pdf |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210719092004/https://digitalhub.fifa.com/m/52463a2cc01d135/original/c91tgtfetbsmneex1vbc-pdf.pdf |archive-date=19 July 2021 |access-date=21 December 2020 |publisher=[[FIFA]] |page=3 |quote=Known as Taiwan before 1982.}}</ref> and competed internationally under the names of both "Republic of China" (in [[Asian Football Confederation|AFC]] and [[International Olympic Committee|IOC]]-organized tournaments; Code: '''ROC'''<ref>{{cite news |title=Moments and Milestones: The 1960 AFC Asian Cup |url=https://www.the-afc.com/en/more/news/moments_and_milestones_the_1960_afc_asian_cup.html |access-date=12 September 2024 |work=the-AFC |language=en |quote=Chinese Taipei (known then as Republic of China)}}</ref>) and "Taiwan" (in FIFA-organized tournaments; Code:'''TAI'''<ref name=":0" />). Due to this inconsistency, from 1954 to 1978, the national team refused to participate in any FIFA-organized events, like FIFA World Cup and its qualifications, where they couldn't compete under the name of "Republic of China". Only in 1982 did the team fully adopt the name "Chinese Taipei".

The national team achieved its greatest success during this early period. It qualified for the [[1960 AFC Asian Cup]] and finished third, its best performance in the tournament to date. In the same year, the team also participated in the football tournament at the [[Football at the 1960 Summer Olympics|1960 Summer Olympics]]. Notably, several players in the squad were originally from [[British Hong Kong|Hong Kong]], despite Hong Kong having its own [[Hong Kong national football team|national team]] at the time.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Archived copy |url=http://www.isu.edu.tw/upload/26/4/HS_journals/Vol1-9/11.pdf |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170810183120/http://www.isu.edu.tw/upload/26/4/HS_journals/Vol1-9/11.pdf |archive-date=2017-08-10 |access-date=2014-01-07}}</ref>

In 1970, an agreement between the CTFA and the [[Hong Kong Football Association]] ended the eligibility of Hong Kong-based players to represent Taiwan. The national team's performance declined significantly thereafter, and it has not qualified for the AFC Asian Cup or the FIFA World Cup since.

=== Participation in Oceania Football Confederation (1975–1989) ===
Due to political conflicts with the PRC, Chinese Taipei was expelled from the [[Asian Football Confederation]] (AFC)<ref>{{cite web |title=NewspaperSG |url=https://eresources.nlb.gov.sg/newspapers/digitised/page/straitstimes19740916-1.1.28 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160919232731/http://eresources.nlb.gov.sg/newspapers/Digitised/Page/straitstimes19740916-1.1.28.aspx |archive-date=19 September 2016 |access-date=25 September 2023}}</ref> and the [[Asian Games]]. As a result, from 1975 to 1989, Chinese Taipei competed as a member of the [[Oceania Football Confederation]] (OFC). During this period, the team struggled to achieve significant results on the international stage.

=== Return to AFC and modern developments (1989–present) ===
Chinese Taipei was re-admitted to the AFC in 1989<ref>{{cite web |title=足協簡介 - 中華民國足球協會Ctfa |url=https://www.ctfa.com.tw/aboutus/ctfa-profile.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231024140721/https://www.ctfa.com.tw/aboutus/ctfa-profile.html |archive-date=24 October 2023 |access-date=25 September 2023}}</ref> and to the [[Olympic Council of Asia]] in 1990.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Pakistan Sports Board, Islamabad |url=http://www.sports.gov.pk/Detail/OTdlMGQ4ZjktODFiZi00YzZhLWI2ZjgtNjY3N2Q3NWM1MGFm |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231010113458/http://www.sports.gov.pk/Detail/OTdlMGQ4ZjktODFiZi00YzZhLWI2ZjgtNjY3N2Q3NWM1MGFm |archive-date=10 October 2023 |access-date=25 September 2023}}</ref> The national team gradually began rebuilding its international presence but remained a minor footballing nation in Asia.{{Cn|date=August 2023}}

In recent years, Chinese Taipei experienced a resurgence in form under English coach [[Gary White (footballer)|Gary White]], who was appointed in 2017.<ref>{{Cite web |title=White takes helm of Taiwan soccer |url=http://www.ebfc.co.uk/news/debut-win-for-hughes-1652552.html |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211011084226/https://www.ebfc.co.uk/news/debut-win-for-hughes-1652552.html |archive-date=11 October 2021 |access-date=2021-02-17 |website=www.ebfc.co.uk}}</ref> White implemented modern tactical approaches and launched a global scouting program to identify players of Taiwanese descent abroad. This initiative brought in players such as [[Tim Chow]], [[Will Donkin]], and [[Emilio Estevez (footballer)|Emilio Estevez]], who contributed to improved performances.{{Cn|date=August 2023}}

In December 2017, Chinese Taipei hosted and won the [[CTFA International Tournament]], featuring [[Laos national football team|Laos]], the [[Philippines national football team|Philippines]], and [[Timor-Leste national football team|Timor-Leste]]. It marked the national team's first official international trophy in 55 years.<ref>{{Cite web |title=足協邀請賽 / 59年首嘗冠軍滋味 中華隊踢出勝利榮耀 - 中華民國足球協會CTFA |url=https://www.ctfa.com.tw/hotnews/ctfa-news/3771-59.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220829205818/https://www.ctfa.com.tw/hotnews/ctfa-news/3771-59.html |archive-date=29 August 2022 |access-date=2021-02-17 |website=www.ctfa.com.tw}}</ref> Forward [[Li Mao]] finished as the tournament's top scorer with four goals.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2017-12-06 |title=Taiwan beat Laos, claim CTFA17 title - Taipei Times |url=https://www.taipeitimes.com/News/sport/archives/2017/12/06/2003683509 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200109123728/http://www.taipeitimes.com/News/sport/archives/2017/12/06/2003683509 |archive-date=9 January 2020 |access-date=2021-02-17 |website=www.taipeitimes.com}}</ref>

Under White's leadership, Chinese Taipei won seven consecutive FIFA-recognized matches and advanced to the [[2019 AFC Asian Cup qualification – third round|third round of qualification]] for the [[2019 AFC Asian Cup]], narrowly missing qualification by one point behind [[Bahrain national football team|Bahrain]] and [[Turkmenistan national football team|Turkmenistan]]. White departed in September 2018 to manage the Hong Kong national team.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Chan |first1=Kin-wa |date=10 September 2018 |title=Gary White lands Hong Kong job as Football Association finally confirm new head coach after months of speculation |url=https://www.scmp.com/sport/hong-kong/article/2163509/gary-white-finally-confirmed-new-head-coach-hong-kong-football |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180911120902/https://www.scmp.com/sport/hong-kong/article/2163509/gary-white-finally-confirmed-new-head-coach-hong-kong-football |archive-date=11 September 2018 |access-date=10 September 2018 |website=South China Morning Post}}</ref>

After White's departure, the team's performance declined. Interim coach [[Vom Ca-nhum]] led the squad in the [[2019 EAFF E-1 Football Championship|EAFF E-1 Championship]] qualifying round, but failed to progress further.{{Cn|date=August 2023}} In 2019, [[Louis Lancaster]], another English coach and former assistant to White, was appointed head coach.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2019-10-14 |title=He moulded one of the world's best young players. Now he'll try to take down the Socceroos |url=https://www.foxsports.com.au/football/socceroos/socceroos-chinese-taipei-vs-australia-preview-analysis-louis-lancaster-jadon-sancho-graham-arnold-robbie-cornthwaite/news-story/d125daec0cfc8c59ac30386217856c0e |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230405042741/https://www.foxsports.com.au/football/socceroos/socceroos-chinese-taipei-vs-australia-preview-analysis-louis-lancaster-jadon-sancho-graham-arnold-robbie-cornthwaite/news-story/d125daec0cfc8c59ac30386217856c0e |archive-date=5 April 2023 |access-date=2021-02-17 |website=Fox Sports |language=en}}</ref> However, the team won only one of nine matches that year and suffered heavy defeats during the early stages of [[2022 FIFA World Cup qualification (AFC)|qualification]] for the [[2022 FIFA World Cup]].{{Cn|date=August 2023}} Lancaster was dismissed in December 2019,<ref>{{Cite web |date=2020-02-01 |title=FEATURE: National soccer suffers horror 2019 - Taipei Times |url=https://www.taipeitimes.com/News/taiwan/archives/2020/02/01/2003730173 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200204152409/http://www.taipeitimes.com/News/taiwan/archives/2020/02/01/2003730173 |archive-date=4 February 2020 |access-date=2021-02-17 |website=www.taipeitimes.com}}</ref> and replaced by Vom Ca-nhum, who held an [[AFC Professional Coaching Diploma|AFC Pro A license]] and was appointed on a permanent basis.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2020-02-15 |title=Men's national soccer team appoints Vom Ca-nhum as side's new manager - Taipei Times |url=https://www.taipeitimes.com/News/sport/archives/2020/02/15/2003731023 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200928230850/http://www.taipeitimes.com/News/sport/archives/2020/02/15/2003731023 |archive-date=28 September 2020 |access-date=2021-02-17 |website=www.taipeitimes.com}}</ref>

In October 2023, Chinese Taipei competed in the [[2026 FIFA World Cup qualification – AFC first round|first round of qualification]] for the [[2026 FIFA World Cup]]. They defeated Timor-Leste 7–0 on aggregate,{{Cn|date=August 2023}} advancing to the [[2026 FIFA World Cup qualification – AFC second round|second round]], where they were drawn into Group D alongside [[Oman national football team|Oman]], [[Kyrgyzstan national football team|Kyrgyzstan]], and [[Malaysia national football team|Malaysia]].{{Cn|date=August 2023}}

== Team image ==

=== Kit ===
As of January 2023, the official kit supplier is local Taiwanese sports brand Entes.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Staff |first=Football Fashion |date=2022-01-27 |title=Chinese Taipei 2022/23 Entes Home and Away Kits |url=https://footballfashion.org/wordpress/2022/01/27/chinese-taipei-2022-23-entes-home-and-away-kits/ |access-date=2023-01-15 |website=FOOTBALL FASHION |language=en-US |archive-date=15 January 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230115121423/https://footballfashion.org/wordpress/2022/01/27/chinese-taipei-2022-23-entes-home-and-away-kits/ |url-status=live }}</ref>

=== Stadium ===
Chinese Taipei played their home matches at the [[Kaohsiung National Stadium]] which is able to hold up to 55,000 seating capacity. The stadium, designed by Japanese architect [[Toyo Ito]], makes use of 1 MW of [[solar cell]]s to provide most of its power needs. The stadium's semi spiral-shaped, like a dragon, is the first stadium in the world to provide power using [[solar power]] technology. The [[solar panel]]s covering the vast external face of the stadium are able to generate most of the power required for its own operation, as well as additional power that can be sent to the grid.

Chinese Taipei used to play their match at the 20,000 capacity stadium [[Taipei Municipal Stadium]] which as of now, will be second in line to host the national team matches. On 3 July 2011, the stadium recorded its highest attendance for a football game when Chinese Taipei hosted Malaysia in the first round of the [[2014 FIFA World Cup qualification - AFC first round|2014 FIFA World Cup qualification]] second leg match, when 15,335 spectators attended the game.

== Coaching staff ==

''As of 14 March 2026''

|-

! style="background:blue;color:white"|Name
|-
|Head coach
|{{ flagicon|AUS}} [[Matt Ross (soccer coach)|Matt Ross]]
|-
|Assistant coach

|Goalkeeping coach
|{{flagicon|GER}} [[Michael Kraft]]
|-
|Physical coach
|{{flagicon|ENG}} Nicholas Punchard

===Coaching history===

[[File:Chen Kuei-jen in Taipower.jpg|thumb|200px|[[Chen Kuei-jen]] is a former manager of the Chinese Taipei national football team]]
[[File:Louis Lancaster Chinese Taipei.jpg|thumb|200px|[[Louis Lancaster]] became the manager of the Chinese Taipei national football team in 2019]]
:[[Caretaker manager]]s are listed in ''italics''.
{{div col|colwidth=25em}}
*{{flagicon|ROC|1929}}{{flagicon|HKG|1910}} [[Ngan Shing-kwan]] (1936)
*{{flagicon|ROC|1929}}{{flagicon|HKG|1910}} [[Lee Wai Tong]] (1954–1958)
* {{flagicon|TWN}}{{flagicon|HKG|1910}} [[Ho Ying Fun]] (1966)
*{{flagicon|TWN}}{{flagicon|HKG|1910}} [[Pau King Yin]] (1966, 1968, 1971)
*{{flagicon|ROC|1929}}{{flagicon|HKG|1910}} [[Hsu King Shing]] (1967)
*{{flagicon|TWN}}{{flagicon|HKG|1959}} [[Law Pak]] (1977–1981)
*{{flagicon|TWN}} [[Chiang Chia]] (1981–1985)
*{{flagicon|TWN}} [[Lo Chih-tsung]] (1985–1988)
*{{flagicon|TWN}} [[Huang Jen-cheng]] (1988–1993)
*{{flagicon|TWN}} [[Chiang Mu-tsai]] (1994–2000)
*{{flagicon|TWN}} [[Huang Jen-cheng]] (2000–2001)
*{{flagicon|TWN}} [[Lee Po-houng]] (2001–2005)
*{{flagicon|BRA}} [[Dido (footballer)|Edson Silva]] (2005)
*{{flagicon|JPN}} [[Toshiaki Imai]] (2005–2007)
*{{flagicon|TWN}} [[Chen Sing-an]] (2008–2009)
*{{flagicon|TWN}} [[Lo Chih-tsung]] (2009–2011)
*{{flagicon|KOR|1997}} [[Lee Tae-ho]] (2011)
*{{flagicon|TWN}} ''[[Chen Kuei-jen]]'' (2012)
*{{flagicon|TWN}} [[Chiang Mu-tsai]] (2012)
*{{flagicon|TWN}} [[Chen Kuei-jen]] (2013–2016)
*{{flagicon|JPN}} [[Toshiaki Imai]] (2016)
*{{flagicon|JPN}} [[Kazuo Kuroda]] (2016–2017)
*{{flagicon|JPN}} ''[[Reiji Hirata]]'' (2017)
*{{flagicon|ENG}} [[Gary White (footballer)|Gary White]] (2017–2018)
*{{flagicon|TWN}} ''[[Vom Ca-nhum]]'' (2018)
*{{flagicon|ENG}} [[Louis Lancaster]] (2019)
*{{flagicon|TWN}} [[Vom Ca-nhum]] (2020–2021)
*{{flagicon|TWN}} ''[[Yeh Hsien-chung]]'' (2021)
*{{flagicon|TWN}} [[Yeh Hsien-chung]] (2022–2023)
*{{flagicon|ENG}} [[Gary White (footballer)|Gary White]] (2023–2025)
* {{flagicon|TWN}} ''[[Chen Sing-an]]'' (2025)
* {{flagicon|TWN}} [[Huang Che-ming]] (2025)
* {{flagicon|AUS}} ''[[Matt Ross (soccer coach)|Matt Ross]]'' (2025–)

{{div col end}}

==Players==
===Current squad===

The following 23 players were called up for the [[2027 AFC Asian Cup qualification – third round]] against [[Sri Lanka national football team|Sri Lanka]] on 31 March 2026.<ref>{{Cite web |title=115年男子國家代表隊2027亞洲盃資格賽最終輪賽前集訓名冊 |url=https://www.ctfa.com.tw/index.php?option=com_content&view=article&id=8168&Itemid=7356 |website=中華民國足球協會CTFA }}</ref>

''Caps and goals updated as of 31 March 2026, after the match against [[Sri Lanka national football team|Sri Lanka]]''.

{{nat fs g start}}
{{nat fs g player|no=1|pos=GK|name=[[Huang Chiu-lin]]|age={{bda|1997|6|18|df=y}}|caps=10|goals=0|club=[[Taiwan Power Company F.C.|Taipower]]|clubnat=TWN}}

{{nat fs end}}

** {{Gold1}} '''Gold medal (2)''': [[Football at the 1954 Asian Games|1954]], [[Football at the 1958 Asian Games|1958]]

* '''[[CTFA International Tournament]] (1)''': 2017
